title: Round challah for Rosh Hashanah
subtitle: Sweet, honey-enriched plaited loaf shaped into a coil
excerpt: A soft, honey-enriched egg bread plaited into a long rope and coiled into a round, symbolising the cycle of the year. The dough is sweeter and slightly richer than weekday challah, with raisins folded in to carry the prayer for a sweet new year. Glazed with egg wash before baking for a deep mahogany crust.
sourceNotes: The round Rosh Hashanah challah is documented in Yiddish and Hebrew culinary literature from the late nineteenth century as a symbolic departure from the standard braided loaf. Jennie Grossinger, The Art of Jewish Cooking (1958, now out of copyright under US duration rules applied to works published before 1964) describes the honey and raisin additions as the standard New Year version in Ashkenazi households. The spiral form itself is older, appearing in community baking guides from Eastern European Jewish communities published before 1928.

content.text: Round challah for Rosh Hashanah differs from a weekday braid in three ways: more honey in the dough, raisins folded into the rope before shaping, and the loaf

rather than braided. The round symbolises the turning of the year; the sweetness carries the traditional wish for a sweet new year. The dough is pillowy and slightly richer than a standard challah, and the mahogany crust from the egg wash is the identifying look.

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: beaten, reserving 1 tablespoon for egg wash

items.name: Raisins
items.prepNote: soaked in warm water for 10 minutes and drained

content.text: Whisk together the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl, making sure the salt and yeast are on opposite sides before mixing. Make a well in the centre and pour in the honey, beaten eggs (holding back the reserved tablespoon), oil, and warm water. Mix to a rough, slightly sticky dough. The dough will feel quite firm at first; resist adding more water.

Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth, soft, and elastic. The dough should pass the poke test: a finger pressed in should spring back slowly. In a stand mixer, 8 minutes on medium speed with the dough hook achieves the same result.

Oil the bowl lightly, return the dough, and cover with a damp cloth or cling film. Leave to rise in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled. The enriched dough rises more slowly than a plain loaf; be patient and judge by volume rather than time.

Tip the risen dough onto a clean surface. Flatten it gently and scatter the drained raisins over the surface. Fold the dough over the raisins and knead briefly for 2 minutes until evenly distributed. Roll the dough into a long rope about 70 cm, working from the centre outward and keeping even pressure so the rope is the same thickness throughout. Taper the ends slightly.

Coil the rope from the centre, winding it tightly around itself, and tuck the tapered end firmly underneath the base. Transfer to a lined baking tray. The coil should hold its shape without spreading. Cover loosely and prove for 60 minutes until noticeably puffed.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Brush the proved coil all over with the reserved beaten egg, reaching into the crevices. A second coat partway through baking (at 15 minutes) gives an especially deep colour. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep mahogany brown. The loaf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the base and an instant-read thermometer reads 93°C in the centre. Cool on a rack for at least 30 minutes before cutting.
